events.text: Let us start with the system view. The machine query command with the system filter shows uptime, disk usage, and storage status.
events.afterText: Uptime, disk, storage. Everything you need at a glance.
events.prose: Start with the system view. rdc machine query with the system filter shows uptime, disk usage, and storage status.

events.text: Now containers. This view lists every running container across every repository on the machine: name, status, health, CPU, memory, plus which repo owns each one.
events.afterText: Every container across every repo, in one view.
events.prose: The containers view lists every running container across every repository on the machine: name, status, health, CPU, memory, and the owning repo.

events.text: And the repos themselves. Size, mount status, Docker daemon status, disk usage. Everything you need to know about each isolated environment.
events.afterText: All repos, all their daemons, all their disk usage.
events.prose: The repositories view reports size, mount status, Docker daemon status, and disk usage for each isolated environment.

events.text: The machine query command with no filters gives you everything in one shot. System info, containers, repositories, all together.
events.prose: Run rdc machine query with no filters to get system info, containers, and repositories in a single output.

events.text: And finally the diagnostic check for a local sanity check on your laptop. Node, SSH key, renet, Docker. All the things the CLI itself depends on. That is the full series. From install to monitoring, all from your terminal.
events.afterText: All green means your local CLI is healthy.
events.prose: Run rdc doctor for a local sanity check on your laptop. It verifies Node, SSH key, renet, and Docker, every dependency the CLI itself relies on.

narrations.text: Alright! Your app is live and backed up, now let's make sure it stays healthy.
The CLI gives you a full picture of any server from your laptop. Three angles to check: system health, running containers, and the repositories themselves.
You can now install, configure, deploy, fork, go live, autostart, back up, and monitor. The advanced track starts next: version your repos like code, with commits and rollbacks.
